does: This paper introduces OpenRubrics, a large-scale collection of prompt-rubric pairs designed to train rubric-based reward models for RLHF. The core contribution is Contrastive Rubric Generation (CRG), a method that automatically derives both hard rules (explicit constraints) and principles (implicit qualities) by contrasting preferred and rejected responses. The authors construct a diverse dataset and implement a filtering process that removes noisy rubrics by preserving preference-label consistency. They train Rubric-RM, a reward model that uses these structured rubrics to evaluate responses across multiple dimensions rather than producing scalar judgments. Across multiple reward-modeling benchmarks, Rubric-RM outperforms size-matched baselines by 8.4%, and these improvements transfer to downstream policy optimization tasks.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in rubrics-as-rewards (RaR) that recognize scalar and pairwise reward judgments inadequately capture multidimensional human preferences. It extends prior research on RLHF and reward modeling by addressing the scalability challenge that has limited rubric-based approaches. The contrastive learning framework draws on established techniques for mining discriminative features by comparing positive and negative examples. The paper responds to limitations in existing reward models used for LLM alignment, which typically provide single-valued outputs. It also connects to broader work on interpretability in reward modeling, since rubrics provide structured, human-readable evaluation criteria rather than opaque learned representations.
enables: This work opens pathways toward more interpretable and nuanced reward modeling for LLM alignment. By demonstrating that rubric-based rewards outperform scalar baselines, it suggests future systems could provide more informative feedback signals to language models during training. The scalable synthetic rubric generation method enables larger and more diverse rubric collections, potentially supporting rubric-based rewards across varied domains and task types. The approach could facilitate better alignment with complex human preferences by decomposing quality judgments into explicit criteria and implicit principles. Additionally, the dataset and generation methodology provide infrastructure for studying how structured evaluation frameworks improve policy learning compared to traditional scalar rewards, opening investigations into human-AI collaboration where rubrics serve as shared evaluation standards.
